questions.stem: What does MPI stand for?
questions.explanation: MPI is the Message Passing Interface, a standard library specification for writing parallel programs in which separate processes communicate by sending and receiving messages.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which function must be called to initialize the MPI environment before any other MPI call?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Init` sets up the MPI execution environment and must be called before almost any other MPI function. It is typically the first MPI call in `main`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which function shuts down the MPI environment at the end of the program?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Finalize` cleans up the MPI environment and must be the last MPI call. After it returns, no further MPI functions may be used.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which function reports the identifier (rank) of the calling process within a communicator?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Comm_rank` returns the rank of the calling process, a unique integer from `0` to one less than the number of processes. Each process uses its rank to decide what work to do.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which function reports the total number of processes in a communicator?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Comm_size` returns how many processes belong to the communicator. With `MPI_COMM_WORLD` this is the total number of processes launched. `MPI_Comm_rank` instead gives the individual process its own id.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: If an MPI program is launched with 4 processes, what range of ranks will `MPI_Comm_rank` return across them?
questions.explanation: Ranks are numbered starting from `0`, so with `4` processes the ranks are `0, 1, 2, 3`. The largest rank is always the process count minus one.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: If an MPI program runs with 8 processes, what value does `MPI_Comm_size` return on `MPI_COMM_WORLD`?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Comm_size` reports the count of processes, which is `8` here. Unlike the rank, the size is the same value on every process.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What is the default communicator that includes all the processes in an MPI program?
questions.explanation: `MPI_COMM_WORLD` is the predefined communicator that contains every process started by the program. Most basic MPI calls use it to refer to the whole group of processes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which pair of functions performs basic point-to-point communication in MPI?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` move a message directly from one specific process to another, which is point-to-point communication. The other pairs are collective operations or setup and teardown calls.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which MPI function sends the same data from one process to all processes in the communicator?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Bcast` broadcasts a single message from the root process to every other process in the communicator, so they all end up with the same data. `MPI_Send` only targets one receiver.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which MPI function distributes different chunks of an array from the root process so each process gets its own portion?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Scatter` splits an array on the root and sends a distinct piece to each process. This differs from `MPI_Bcast`, which sends the same data to everyone rather than dividing it.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which MPI function collects pieces of data from all processes and assembles them on the root process?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Gather` is the inverse of `MPI_Scatter`: each process contributes its data and the root receives all the pieces combined into one array. `MPI_Scatter` goes the other way, from root out to the processes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which MPI function combines values from all processes using an operation such as sum or maximum?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Reduce` takes one value from each process and combines them with a reduction operation like `MPI_SUM` or `MPI_MAX`, delivering the single result to the root. The other functions move data without combining it.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Using `MPI_Reduce` with the operation `MPI_SUM`, if four processes contribute the values `1`, `2`, `3`, and `4`, what result arrives at the root?
questions.explanation: `MPI_SUM` adds the contributed values together: `1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10`. The combined result is delivered to the root process. `MPI_PROD` would instead multiply them.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Using `MPI_Reduce` with the operation `MPI_MAX`, if processes contribute `7`, `2`, `9`, and `4`, what result arrives at the root?
questions.explanation: `MPI_MAX` keeps the largest contributed value, which is `9`. A reduction with `MPI_MAX` finds the maximum across all processes, not the sum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What is a collective communication operation in MPI?
options: A call that only the root may make alone
A way to print to the screen
A message between exactly two processes
An operation that all processes in a communicator participate in together, such as `MPI_Bcast` or `MPI_Reduce`
questions.explanation: Collective operations involve every process in the communicator, which must all call the function. Examples include `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Scatter`, `MPI_Gather`, and `MPI_Reduce`. Point-to-point calls like `MPI_Send` involve only two processes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: In a typical MPI program, what is the correct overall order of these calls?
questions.explanation: Every MPI program starts with `MPI_Init`, then does its work including calls like `MPI_Comm_rank`, and ends with `MPI_Finalize`. Calling rank queries before initialization or skipping these setup and teardown calls is incorrect.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: In `MPI_Send(&data, 1, MPI_INT, 2, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);`, what does the argument `2` specify?
options: The number of items to send
The rank of the destination process
questions.explanation: In `MPI_Send`, after the buffer, count, and datatype comes the destination rank, here `2`, then the tag, then the communicator. So `2` is the rank of the process that should receive the message. The count is the separate `1`, and the tag is the `0`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: In the SPMD model used by MPI, how can different processes do different work even though they run the same program?
options: The compiler makes separate programs automatically
They each load a different source file
They branch on their rank, so code can run only on certain ranks
questions.explanation: MPI follows the Single Program Multiple Data model: every process runs the same executable but examines its rank from `MPI_Comm_rank` and uses `if` statements on that rank to take different paths. For example, `if (rank == 0)` can designate the root.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which MPI operation is the logical inverse of `MPI_Scatter`?
questions.explanation: `MPI_Scatter` sends pieces of data from the root out to all processes, and `MPI_Gather` reverses that by collecting a piece from each process back onto the root. They form a natural distribute-then-collect pair.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: For `MPI_Recv` to accept a message from `MPI_Send`, which of these generally must match between them?
options: The communicator, and the source, tag, and datatype must be compatible
The line numbers in the source code
The variable names used in each process
questions.explanation: A send and a matching receive must use the same communicator and have compatible source or destination ranks, tags, and datatypes so MPI can pair them. The actual variable names are irrelevant; only the message envelope matters.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which MPI datatype constant corresponds to the C type `int`?
questions.explanation: `MPI_INT` is the MPI datatype matching the C `int`. MPI needs the datatype argument so it can handle the message correctly across processes. `MPI_FLOAT`, `MPI_CHAR`, and `MPI_DOUBLE` correspond to `float`, `char`, and `double`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: After `MPI_Bcast` runs with the root holding the value `100`, what value do the other processes hold for that variable?
questions.explanation: A broadcast copies the root's data to all processes, so every process ends up with `100`. That is the whole purpose of `MPI_Bcast`: to give all participants the same value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Identify the MPI setup and query calls.
questions.solution: The function that initializes the MPI environment, called first, is {{1}}. The function that finalizes it, called last, is {{2}}. The function a process uses to learn its own rank is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `MPI_Init` starts the environment, `MPI_Finalize` shuts it down, and `MPI_Comm_rank` tells a process its own rank within the communicator.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Reason about ranks and size for a run.
questions.solution: An MPI program is launched with `6` processes on `MPI_COMM_WORLD`. The value returned by `MPI_Comm_size` is {{1}}. The smallest rank any process can have is {{2}}, and the largest rank is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: With `6` processes, `MPI_Comm_size` returns `6`. Ranks start at `0` and run to one less than the size, so they span `0` to `5`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Match each collective to what it does.
questions.solution: The collective that sends identical data from the root to every process is {{1}}. The collective that splits an array and gives each process a different piece is {{2}}. The collective that combines one value per process with an operation like sum is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `MPI_Bcast` copies the same data to all processes, `MPI_Scatter` distributes distinct pieces of an array, and `MPI_Reduce` combines a value from each process into a single result.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Predict the results of reductions.
questions.solution: Four processes contribute the values `5`, `8`, `2`, and `5` to a single `MPI_Reduce`. With the operation `MPI_SUM`, the root receives {{1}}. With the operation `MPI_MAX`, the root receives {{2}}. With the operation `MPI_MIN`, the root receives {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `MPI_SUM` adds the contributions: `5 + 8 + 2 + 5 = 20`. `MPI_MAX` selects the largest value, `8`. `MPI_MIN` selects the smallest, `2`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Identify point-to-point calls and the default communicator.
questions.solution: The MPI call that sends a message to one specific process is {{1}}, and the matching call that receives it is {{2}}. The predefined communicator that contains all processes in the program is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` are the basic point-to-point pair for one sender and one receiver. `MPI_COMM_WORLD` is the default communicator holding every process.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Trace gather and broadcast outcomes.
questions.solution: Three processes (ranks 0, 1, 2) each hold their rank as a value and call `MPI_Gather` to the root. The root receives a total of {{1}} values. Afterward the root broadcasts the number `99` with `MPI_Bcast`, so the value held by process rank 2 becomes {{2}}. The number of processes that end up with `99` after the broadcast is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `MPI_Gather` collects one value from each of the three processes, so the root assembles `3` values. `MPI_Bcast` then copies `99` to every process, so rank 2 holds `99` and all `3` processes share that value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Recall MPI datatypes and the SPMD idea.
questions.solution: The MPI datatype constant that matches the C `int` is {{1}}. The MPI datatype that matches the C `double` is {{2}}. In MPI's execution model, all processes run the same program but branch on their {{3}} to do different work.
questions.explanation: `MPI_INT` and `MPI_DOUBLE` are the datatypes for C `int` and `double`. Under the SPMD model, processes run one program and use their rank to choose different code paths.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)
